coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and drug detection performance of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Jingjing
Gao, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Recently, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have attracted considerable interest since nitrogen (N) doping could effectively tailor the electronic properties and the chemical reactivity of carbon dots (CDs) for advanced potential applications. Herein, a one-step pyrolysis method was presented for synthesizing the NCDs with excellent water solubility, good stability and a high quantum yield of ca. 28%. The detection performance of NCDs for the antibacterial drugs was further explored, and it was proved to effectively enhance the fluorescence due to the strong interaction between the NCDs and antibacterial drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent properties
Drug detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (inset shows the HRTEM image of a random NCD) (a) and their size distribution (b) for NCDs.
Absorption spectrum of NCDs (a) and the optical images of NCDs under visible light (b) and UV lamp (365nm, center) (c) illumination.
PL emission spectra (recorded from 320 to 420nm) (a) and the effect of excitation wavelength on the differential PL emission peak of NCDs (b), (c) PL excitation and emission spectra of the NCDs.

(a) PL emission spectra of NCDs mixing with different concentrations of amoxicillin, (b) effect of reaction time of NCDs and amoxicillin (1mL) on the differential PL emission spectra, excited at 365nm and 400nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Synthesis and drug detection performance of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional and Environment Materials Research Institute, College of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional and Environment Materials Research Institute
College of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Structure and Low Dimensional Physics Laboratory, Peking University, Beijing 100871, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nano Structure and Low Dimensional Physics Laboratory

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have attracted considerable interest since nitrogen (N) doping could effectively tailor the electronic properties and the chemical reactivity of carbon dots (CDs) for advanced potential applications. Herein, a one-step pyrolysis method was presented for synthesizing the NCDs with excellent water solubility, good stability and a high quantum yield of ca. 28%. The detection performance of NCDs for the antibacterial drugs was further explored, and it was proved to effectively enhance the fluorescence due to the strong interaction between the NCDs and antibacterial drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, a new fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) (<10nm), has attracted considerable attention due to their preliminary applications in photocatalysis, bioimaging and biomedicine [1–3]. The CDs exhibit distinctively tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility and extraordinary biochemical features [4–9]. For further enhancing the versatile properties of CDs and enriching their potential applications, chemical doping is commonly used to tailor the electronic properties and chemical reactivity of carbonaceous nanomaterial [1,2]. Nitrogen (N) is considered to be an excellent chemical doping element, and the presence of lone pair electrons of N and the commonly introducing defects sites from N doping would provide the possible active sites so as to produce new phenomena and unexpected properties [10–13]. Till date, N-doped carbon nanostructures have been synthesized primarily based on the pyrolysis N-rich organic precursors, including the microwave [14], hydrothermal, solvothermal route [2,15] and solid-phase reaction strategy [16]. The pyrolysis method had advantages such as plentiful raw material sources, low cost as well as the simple synthetic conditions [14,15].

Antibacterial medicaments, with the antibacterial properties which could strengthen the body's immune system, are one of the major resources for the healthcare. Amoxicillin ( Scheme 1) is one of the mostly-used drugs for the treatment of the bacterial infections. However, despite its apparent innocuous character, the existence of negative effects such as the hepatic and renal dysfunctions would be potentially harmful to human body. Therefore, efficient ways for strictly mastering the dose and concentration of the amoxicillin are highly desirable. Semiconductor quantum dots (such as CdS) have been shown to be applied in diverse fluorescence detection and used as biochemical probes in the field of biological detection [17–19]. However, carbon based nanomaterials, with small size, high stability, non-toxic side effects and good biocompatibility, would be more attractive for organisms in the biological systems as well as in the disease diagnosis and treatment [20–22]. In recent years, many efforts have been devoted to exploring the potential biological applications of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [23,24]. It also reported that carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) were successfully used as the fluorescent probes for the determination of quercetin [25].

Herein, in this paper, we employed glutamic acid (Glu) as the precursor to prepare photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) via a facile pyrolysis method. By using this method, nitrogen and carbon atoms decomposed from Glu would simultaneously form the NCDs. And besides exploring the optical properties, the detection performance of as-prepared NCDs for antibacterial drugs such as amoxicillin was further exploited. The results reveal that the introduction of amoxicillin would effectively enhance the fluorescence of NCDs.

The NCDs were synthesized by a one-step thermal conversion method. 2.0g Glu (l-Glutamic acid, A.R.) was heated to 200°C using a heating mantle (SZCL-2 Digital display and intelligent temperature controlling magnetic stirrer). After 5min., the sample was liquated, and the color of the liquid changed from colorless to brown. Then the obtained liquid was added dropwise into 50mL of 0.01gmL−1 NaOH solution under violent stirring till the pH of aqueous solution reached 7.

In a typical process for the detection of amoxicillin, 10mL of the aqueous solution of NCDs was poured into four test tubes, which were then mixed with different volumes (1mL, 3mL, 5mL, and 7mL) of aqueous solution of amoxicillin (0.5mgmL−1), separately. The as-produced samples were finally placed at room temperature for an appropriate period of time.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was examined under an F-30 S-TWIN electron microscope (Tecnai G2, FEI Company). The photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra were measured with an FLS-920T fluorescence spectrophotometer fitted with a Xe900 lamp (450W). Diffuse reflectance ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was obtained using a Perkin-Elmer 950 spectrometer. The UV light irradiation was a low-pressure Hg lamp with power 12W. All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

TEM characterization of the NCDs is shown in Fig. 1a. Most of the NCDs are spherical and some of them are elliptical. Fig. 1b shows the size distribution and the average size of NCDs was ca. 4.6nm. It can be easily observed that some NCDs are connected with each other, and the phenomenon would be probably ascribed to the inhomogeneous pyrolysis process [1,14,26,27]. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (the inset of Fig. 1a) image shows the high crystallinity of the NCDs with the lattice spacing of ca. 0.295nm, which agrees with the (002) spacing of graphite [2].

The UV–vis absorption and detailed PL spectra were carried out to investigate the optical properties of the as-prepared NCDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, the absorption spectrum of the NCDs shows an absorption peak at ca. 310nm, which would represent the n→π⁎ transition of C=O [28]. The featureless profile absorption indicates that the products would belong to the indirect bandgap semiconductor nanoparticles, suggesting that the NCDs would be potential candidates for the effective light-emitting and targeted tracing [29]. The NCDs solution is highly transparent in ambient light. While under UV irradiation, the bright blue luminescence can be clearly observed (Fig. 2b and c). In order to further explore their PL properties, the detailed PL emission spectra were studied under excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3a, like most of the reported CDs and NCDs [1,2,6,26,30,31], the as-prepared NCDs also exhibit the distinctive excitation dependent PL behavior. When the products were excited from 320 to 420nm, the corresponding PL emission peaks shifted from 461nm to 487nm. Moreover, the intensity of the emission peaks becomes strong and then weak with the increasing excitation lengths (as shown in Fig. 3b). The highest blue emission intensity with a quantum yield as high as ca. 28% was obtained when excited at 400nm. The PL excitation and emission spectra are shown in Fig. 3c. It can be seen that the NCDs exhibit strong emission at 470nm when it was excited at 400nm. And the excitation spectrum monitored at 470nm shows an excitation peak with a maximum value of 400nm. The observed distinctive luminescence emissions should be closely related to the N doping. The excitation-dependent PL behavior of CDs would not only origin from the NCDs with different sizes but also from the inhomogeneous distribution of emissive sites due to the N doping [2,30].

In order to exploit the potential applications of the produced NCDs, the NCDs were directly applied in the detection of amoxicillin without any further functionalization. Different concentrations of the aqueous solution of amoxicillin were injected into the NCDs solution, and 24h later, the PL properties of the samples were measured. As shown in Fig. 4a, the five emission curves from down (black) to up (pink) represents for the pure NCDs and NCDs mixing with 1mL, 3mL, 5mL and 7mL amoxicillin (0.5mgmL−1). It can be easily observed that amoxicillin can strengthen the luminescent intensity of NCDs as well as broaden the emission peak. Interestingly, the emission peaks also show an obvious blue-shift with increasing the concentration of amoxicillin. We speculated that there are two important factors attributed to the enhancement of the fluorescence when adding amoxicillin into the NCDs solution. One is the electrostatic repulsion between the amoxicillin adsorbed on the surface of the NCDs, which would increase the distance between the NCDs. Normally, the aggregation of quantum dots would decrease the PL quantum yields due to the energy transfer between them. The amoxicillin molecules would effectively separate the NCDs from each other, and then decrease the nonradiative transition. Besides that, the amoxicillin as the ligand would eliminate the surface defects of the NCDs, and finally enhance the fluorescence of the NCDs [32–34]. And the mechanism still needs to be investigated for the further research. From the results of Fig. 4b, we could observe that the enhancement effect is not increased over time. According to the above results, the obtained NCDs have promising applications in biomedicine and can detect antibacterial drugs sensitively by fluorescence spectra.

In summary, we have presented a one-step pyrolysis method of N-doped fluorescence CDs by using glutamic acid as the precursor. It would be concluded that the as-synthesized NCDs with small size exhibit high quantum yield and bright blue PL emission under UV light irradiation. It also reveals that amoxicillin could effectively enhance the fluorescence of NCDs. These discoveries make the NCDs potential probes in biological detection and could facilitate fundamental studies and more potential biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2013-186) and the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant nos. 1208RJYA005 and1208RJYA047).
